original_hypothesis.title: Guard Completely Bypassed When Hook Is Active — Rented Assets Transferable
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In Guard.checkTransaction, when a hook is registered and active for a target contract address, execution flow is forwarded entirely to the hook via _forwardToHook and the _checkTransaction function is NOT called. This means all standard protections (blocking NFT transfers, approvals, module changes, guard removal) are completely delegated to the hook contract. If a hook has any bug, missing check, or doesn't replicate all guard protections, rented assets can be transferred out of the safe. The guard provides ZERO fallback enforcement when a hook is active.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The if/else branch at lines where hook != address(0) && isActive clearly shows _forwardToHook is called and _checkTransaction is in the else branch, meaning they are mutually exclusive. A single buggy hook compromises all guard protections for that target.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (hook != address(0) && isActive) { _forwardToHook(...); } else { _checkTransaction(...); } — mutually exclusive branches, hook bypasses all standard checks

preconditions: GUARD_ADMIN has set a hook for a target contract address
The hook contract does not replicate all Guard._checkTransaction protections
Renter initiates a transaction from the rental safe to the hooked target

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Hook receives onTransaction call but no minimum enforcement requirements exist in the protocol
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Renter calls transfer/approve on hooked target; hook doesn't block it; _checkTransaction never runs
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Full theft of rented NFTs if hook doesn't independently implement rental asset protection

economic_questions: What is the expected interface contract for hooks? Do they receive enough data to enforce rental checks?
Are there deployed hooks and do they replicate guard protections?
Can a renter target a hooked contract that is also a rented NFT's contract?
validation_requirements: Deploy a hook that doesn't revert, verify rented NFT can be transferred via hooked target
Test calling setGuard or enableModule on hooked targets
Verify hooks receive sufficient state to enforce rental checks

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of renting an NFT whose contract has an active hook

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When a hook is set for the rented NFT's contract

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Lender who provided the NFT for rental
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All NFTs rented through hooked contract targets

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Enumerate all deployed hooks and their protection implementations
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm _checkTransaction is unreachable when hook is active

validation_result.mechanism: hook bypasses all guard checks (mutually exclusive branches)
validation_result.impact_summary: When a hook is active for a target contract, all standard Guard protections (blocking NFT transfers, approvals, module/guard changes) are completely bypassed. A buggy or incomplete hook allows rented asset theft.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The Guard.checkTransaction function contains an if/else branch where the hook path and the standard _checkTransaction path are mutually exclusive. When hook != address(0) && isActive evaluates to true, _forwardToHook is called and _checkTransaction is never executed. The _forwardToHook function merely calls IHook(hook).onTransaction() and if it doesn't revert, the transaction is unconditionally allowed. There is no minimum enforcement layer—no fallback check for setGuard, enableModule, disableModule, or NFT transfer/approval selectors. The protocol provides zero interface enforcement on hook contracts to ensure they replicate guard protections. This is a code-level design defect: the guard's core invariant (protecting rented assets from unauthorized transfer) is entirely delegated to an external contract with no minimum safety guarantees. Even a well-intentioned hook set by a trusted GUARD_ADMIN for legitimate purposes (e.g., enabling game interactions) could fail to block NFT transfers if it doesn't independently implement rental-asset protection logic.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The mutually exclusive branches are clearly present in Guard.sol's checkTransaction function. The _forwardToHook function confirms that it only checks whether the hook reverts—no additional guard logic runs. The _checkTransaction function contains all critical protection logic (NFT transfer blocking, approval blocking, module/guard change blocking) but is completely unreachable when a hook is active.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Mutually exclusive if/else: when a hook is active, _checkTransaction (which contains ALL rental asset protections—blocking transfers, approvals, setGuard, enableModule, disableModule) is completely skipped. The hook receives full decision authority with no minimum enforcement.

The hook call is wrapped in try/catch—if hook.onTransaction() doesn't revert, the transaction is unconditionally permitted. No additional guard checks are performed after the hook call. A hook that simply returns (doesn't revert) allows ANY transaction through, including NFT transfers, setGuard, and module changes.

This function contains ALL the guard's rental protection logic: blocking ERC721/ERC1155 transfers and approvals for rented assets, blocking setGuard, enforcing whitelisted modules. When a hook is active for the target, this entire function is unreachable.

calling_sequence: 1. Safe initiates a transaction, triggering Guard.checkTransaction as the installed guard
2. Guard checks delegate call whitelist and minimum 4-byte data requirement
3. Guard fetches hook via STORE.contractToHook(to) and checks STORE.hookOnTransaction(hook)
4. If hook != address(0) && isActive: enters _forwardToHook branch, skipping _checkTransaction entirely
5. _forwardToHook calls IHook(hook).onTransaction(safe, to, value, data) in a try block
6. If hook.onTransaction() does not revert, the checkTransaction returns successfully, allowing the transaction
7. _checkTransaction (which blocks NFT transfers, approvals, setGuard, module changes) is NEVER called
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When GUARD_ADMIN has set a hook for a target contract (e.g., an NFT contract address), any transaction from a rental safe to that target is routed exclusively through the hook. The guard's if/else branch at the end of checkTransaction makes _forwardToHook and _checkTransaction mutually exclusive. If the hook's onTransaction implementation doesn't independently check for ERC721.transferFrom, ERC721.approve, setApprovalForAll, setGuard, enableModule, or disableModule selectors, those operations will succeed. There is no fallback enforcement layer. A renter can call transferFrom on a hooked NFT contract address, and if the hook doesn't block it, the rented NFT leaves the safe.

deployment_assumptions_checked: GUARD_ADMIN role is granted to deployer in DeployProtocol.s.sol
config_validation.deployment_evidence: DeployProtocol.s.sol grants GUARD_ADMIN to deployer: _grantRole('GUARD_ADMIN', deployer). This confirms hooks can be configured. No hooks are set during initial deployment, but the capability exists and is the core concern.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario: (1) GUARD_ADMIN sets a hook for NFT contract X (e.g., to enable staking or game interactions). (2) Hook implements custom logic for game functions but doesn't replicate ALL _checkTransaction protections. (3) Renter rents an NFT from contract X which is held in their rental safe. (4) Renter initiates a transaction from the safe calling X.transferFrom(safe, attacker, tokenId). (5) Guard.checkTransaction fetches the hook for address X, finds it active. (6) Guard calls _forwardToHook which calls hook.onTransaction(). (7) Hook doesn't check for transferFrom selector, doesn't revert. (8) Transaction is allowed, NFT is transferred out of the safe. (9) _checkTransaction which would have caught this via _revertSelectorOnActiveRental was never called. The lender's NFT is stolen.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL: Any rented NFT whose contract address has an active hook is at risk if the hook doesn't perfectly replicate all guard protections. This includes protection against: ERC721 transfers (transferFrom, safeTransferFrom), ERC721 approvals (approve), ERC1155 transfers, setApprovalForAll, setGuard removal, and module enable/disable. A single missing check in any hook compromises all rentals for assets on that hooked contract. The guard's fundamental purpose—protecting rented assets—is completely undermined for hooked targets.
recommended_mitigations: Apply _checkTransaction ALWAYS, regardless of whether a hook is active. Make the hook an additional check, not a replacement: call _forwardToHook AND _checkTransaction when a hook is active.
At minimum, enforce critical invariants (setGuard, enableModule, disableModule blocking) outside the if/else branch so they apply regardless of hook state.
Add an interface requirement for hooks that mandates they call back into the guard for minimum safety verification.
Implement checkAfterExecution to verify post-transaction invariants: guard still set, modules unchanged, rented assets still in safe.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy full protocol. 2. Create a mock hook contract that implements IHook.onTransaction() as a no-op (just returns). 3. As GUARD_ADMIN, call updateHookPath(nftContractAddress, mockHook) and updateHookStatus(mockHook, 1) to enable onTransaction. 4. Create a rental with an NFT from nftContractAddress held in a rental safe. 5. From the rental safe, call nftContract.transferFrom(safe, attacker, tokenId). 6. Verify the transaction succeeds and NFT is transferred out. 7. Verify that without the hook, the same transaction would be blocked by _checkTransaction.
uncertainties: Whether any hooks have been deployed in production and what protections they implement
Whether GUARD_ADMIN would realistically set a hook for an NFT contract that also holds rented assets
The exact line numbers in Guard.sol depend on formatting—the pattern is confirmed but precise lines may shift
additional_testing_needed: Test with a hook that implements partial protections (e.g., blocks transfers but not approvals) to verify partial bypass
Test whether setGuard can be called through a hooked target to remove the guard entirely
Test enableModule/disableModule through a hooked target to verify module protection bypass
Enumerate all deployed hooks in production (if any) and audit their onTransaction implementations
